response:
My heart is racing and I keep second-guessing myself, but I need to just lay out what I see.

Okay, so now Dana is pointing at me hard, saying my nervousness is an act and that I benefited most from Harper's elimination. But Dana was one of the first to pile onto Harper yesterday, and now we have four people left. If I were Mafia, why would I start a theory that got Harper eliminated, putting a target on myself? That would be a huge risk.

What really stands out is Dana's sudden aggressive push against me while barely mentioning Jordan or Bailey. It feels like Dana wants to focus attention on me because I've been visibly anxious and make an easy target. Jordan's hesitation yesterday bugs me, but at least Jordan isn't actively trying to redirect onto me—Dana is.

I also notice Bailey has stayed quiet and neutral the whole time, and that could be calculated, but Bailey's vote was consistent with the group. Dana, on the other hand, has been involved in every accusation shift—first Harper, now me. That pattern of following the momentum and then leading the charge the next day feels suspicious.

I'm scared I'm wrong, but my gut says Dana.

VOTE: Dana

Let me lay out the evidence carefully.

Finley started the Harper theory, which got Harper eliminated. Now Ellis—who backed Finley—is dead. If Finley were Mafia, killing a supporter makes little sense, unless they're trying to look clean by losing an ally. But that's a complex play, and Finley's nervousness seems genuine.

Dana was quick to pile on Harper yesterday, and now is aggressively pointing at Finley while barely mentioning Jordan or me. That's a pattern of shifting focus—first loud players, then Harper, now Finley. Dana's chaos-seeking personality makes them hard to read, but the constant redirection is suspicious.

Jordan waited until the very end to vote Harper, the only player Harper specifically called out before dying. That hesitation could be reluctance to vote a teammate. Now Jordan is pointing at Dana while ignoring me almost entirely—which is convenient.

Here's what matters most: I've been consistent, measured, and voted with the group both times. If I were Mafia, I'd have killed someone suspicious of me, not Ellis who agreed with my vote. The Mafia killed Ellis to silence someone who was vocal against Harper—that weakens Finley's position and strengthens whoever benefits most. Dana's been redirecting all day, and Jordan's been hesitant. Between them, Dana's aggressive pivot onto Finley feels like a desperate attempt to frame someone before the numbers shrink further. I'm voting Dana.

VOTE: Dana

Okay, hold up. Finley's voting me while saying I'm "aggressively pushing" and "redirecting" — but let's look at the actual facts. Finley started the Harper theory, got Harper eliminated (who turned out innocent), and now both Ellis and Harper are dead. Ellis was Finley's biggest backer, and killing them makes Finley look cleaner while eliminating a witness who could've caught them in a lie later.

Bailey's been super consistent, I'll give them that—but being consistent doesn't mean innocent. Jordan waited until the last second to vote Harper, the exact person Harper called out before dying. That hesitation screams reluctance to eliminate a teammate.

But here's my biggest issue: Finley kicked off this whole chain. If I'm Mafia, why would I vote Harper with everyone else and then target Finley today? That makes zero sense—I'd want to keep my head down. Finley's nervous act is perfectly Mafia: start a theory, get a townie killed, let others carry the momentum, then point fingers when heat comes back. Bailey's following Finley's lead, which makes me wonder if they're in cahoots. I'm voting Finley—the origin of this entire mess.

VOTE: Finley

I've been thinking through every angle, and here's where I land.

Finley started the Harper theory that got an innocent eliminated, but honestly, that's equally likely to be a genuine mistake from a nervous villager as it is a Mafia setup. If Finley were Mafia, risking starting a theory that could backfire seems bold but possible.

Bailey has been consistently neutral and measured—voting with the group both times without ever leading a charge. That's either a smart townie or a Mafia player perfectly blending in.

Dana, though—Dana was one of the first to pile onto Harper yesterday, and now today Dana is aggressively targeting Finley while framing it as "Finley started this mess." The shift is noticeable: Dana rode the Harper bandwagon, then when Ellis died, immediately pivoted to blame the person who started that bandwagon. That's convenient redirection. Also, Dana barely mentioned me or Bailey except to dismiss us.

If I look at who benefits most from Ellis's death—it's someone who wants to pin everything on Finley while avoiding scrutiny themselves. Dana's been the loudest voice today, and that push feels like desperation as numbers shrink. I'm voting Dana.

VOTE: Dana

With three players left, I need one more elimination to reach parity and win. Finley has been consistently suspicious of me and has driven the narrative both rounds—first against Harper, now against Dana. Jordan has been more hesitant and cautious, which makes them easier to manipulate or frame tomorrow. Killing Finley removes the sharpest remaining threat and leaves Jordan as a perfect target to blame for everything.

ACTION: Kill Finley
